text:
# Cyber resilience of entrepreneurs lags behind reality

[Report](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/reports/index.html)

[Cross-sector](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/alle-sectoren/index.html)

21/05/2025 • 3 minutes reading

[Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/julia-krauwer/index.html)

Nearly two in ten Dutch companies suffered damage from cybercrime in the past year, according to research by ABN AMRO and MWM2 among entrepreneurs. Three in ten of the large companies suffered damage. Financial losses were the most common, followed by the loss of customer data or trade secrets, and operational disruptions. Yet SMEs in particular overestimate their own cyber resilience.

## Download the complete report

Read all the facts and developments in the report 'Cyber resilience in the Netherlands lags behind geopolitical reality'.

- [Cyber resilience in the Netherlands lags behind geopolitical reality

  2 MB](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/cybertrends-rapport-mei-2025_tcm16-264293.pdf)

Almost all companies with employees surveyed indicate that they have been victims of at least one cyber incident. In large companies this was 93 percent, compared to 85 percent of SMEs and 67 percent of self-employed people (self-employed).

Interestingly enough, organizations have strong confidence in their ability to get and keep their own cyber security in order. A quarter of them find this “not difficult at all”, and 55 percent “a little difficult”. The smaller the organization, the more optimistic the attitude.

That self-confidence is at odds with reality. The figures for SMEs and self-employed people indicate vulnerability rather than control. For example, they focus on securing devices, firewalls and antivirus programs, but often lack proactive detection of intrusions or plans for recovery after a hack. Larger companies, on the other hand, take a broader set of measures and work from the realistic assumption that they will be affected (again) at some point.

When asked about the specific source of the threat to the organization, cyber criminals are mainly mentioned (52 percent of respondents), followed by individual hackers (24 percent) and their own employees (13 percent). State actors were mentioned least, by only 9 percent of organizations.

However, current events show a different picture. At the end of April, dozens of Dutch websites were attacked by a pro-Russian hacker group, in retaliation for military support to Ukraine. The boundaries between cybercriminals and state actors are also blurring. For example, governments are increasingly using access obtained via criminal third parties ('initial access brokers'), hiring commercial hackers, or having cyber attacks carried out via apparently independent groups of ideologically motivated hackers ('hacktivists'). In Russia and Iran, there are strong indications that ransomware groups are consciously tolerated or even controlled by intelligence services.

In a world where countries and companies are strongly digitally connected and power is also exercised digitally, the European Union has opted for a clear strategy. It wants to be less dependent on foreign technology and better protect its own market against cyber threats. An example of this is the NIS2 directive, which requires a wide range of organizations to raise their cyber resilience to a minimum level – from the energy sector and food industry to transport companies and digital services.

Although the directive will be enshrined in Dutch law during the course of this year, NIS2 has not yet been implemented everywhere. Two-thirds of large companies say they are aware of the new legislation, but for SME organizations this is less than half. That is not much, especially considering that companies subject to NIS2 must also have an overview of the digital vulnerabilities of suppliers, customers and partners.

The majority of these companies believe they are largely compliant, although more than half still need to take the final steps to become fully compliant. The intention and direction from Europe are clear, but in practice there is work to be done to tackle the broad threat picture.

## Interview with Julia Krauwer at BNR Nieuwsradio

Listen to the fragment with Sector Banker Julia Krauwer about the cyber resilience of entrepreneurs.
